metadata.file_name: 2. Zhu et al. (2025). Predictive insights into U.S. students’ mathematics performance on PISA 2022 using ensemble tree-based machine learning models.pdf
metadata.title: Predictive insights into U.S. students’ mathematics performance on PISA 2022 using ensemble tree-based machine learning models

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2022 microdata for the United States and merges student and school files using the school identifier (CNTSCHID), assigning school-level predictors to students within the same school. The analytic dataset contains 4552 students in 154 schools, with outcomes defined as the 10 PISA mathematics plausible values (PV1MATH–PV10MATH). For preprocessing, the authors remove predictors with >50% missingness, drop identical and redundant predictors (including removing components used to compute ESCS), and then impute remaining missing predictor values using missForest (RF-based imputation). Although the authors discuss that sampling weights might be less critical for ML, they explicitly state that they did not include sampling weights in their analysis, despite PISA’s complex multistage clustered design.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA 2022 data for 15-year-old students in the United States; merged student and school files via CNTSCHID so that school-level predictors were assigned to students within the same school. Outcomes were the 10 mathematics plausible values (PV1MATH–PV10MATH), and all 10 PVs were included as separate outcome variables with results averaged across PVs. Predictors were filtered by removing variables with >50% missing values, dropping identical predictors (same values for all students), and removing redundant predictors (e.g., retaining ESCS while excluding the three component DVs used to compute ESCS). Remaining missing predictor values were imputed using missForest, and the dataset was split into training (80%, N=3644) and test (20%, N=908) sets.

main_findings.performance_metrics: Model comparison (full vs reduced predictors): XGBoost reduced model rMSE=66.39 (full model rMSE=60.47) with 10 selected predictors; training time=4.14 s. Best overall by accuracy+efficiency: XGBoost (rMSE=66.39 vs LightGBM 66.36 and RF 69.82; training time 4.14 s vs LightGBM 22.93 s and RF 129.27 s).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics data for U.S. students, the study leveraged mathematics self-efficacy, ESCS, expected occupation status, familiarity with math concepts, and family support for self-directed learning to predict mathematics achievement (PVs), finding that these predictors were consistently among the most influential across ensemble tree models and that XGBoost provided the best accuracy–efficiency tradeoff. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzed PISA 2022 mathematics performance for U.S. students (N=4552) using the 10 mathematics plausible values (PV1MATH–PV10MATH) as outcomes. After filtering predictors (>50% missingness removal, dropping identical/redundant variables) and imputing remaining missing values with missForest, the authors trained three ensemble tree models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM) with 5-fold cross-validation and repeated analyses across PVs, averaging results. All three models achieved high predictive accuracy using only a reduced set of ML-selected predictors rather than all predictors; for the reduced models, rMSE was 69.82 (RF, 5 predictors), 66.39 (XGBoost, 10 predictors), and 66.36 (LightGBM, 10 predictors). Considering both accuracy and efficiency, XGBoost was selected as best (training time 4.14 s vs 22.93 s for LightGBM and 129.27 s for RF). The analysis explicitly omitted sampling weights, which may limit generalizability under PISA’s complex clustered design.
